html:
[2426]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 伺察 - To spy on or observe secretly.
  • 乐谱 - Refers to a musical score or notation.
  • 姿容妍美 - Describes someone as having beautiful appearance and grace.
  • 秀出藩邸 - Means to stand out among the nobility or to be exceptionally distinguished among the princes.
  • 钟爱 - Means to favor greatly or to dote on.
  • 聪悟敏慧 - Describes someone as intelligent, quick-witted, and wise.
  • 游幸 - Refers to the emperor's travels or outings.
  • 顷刻不舍 - Means not leaving for a moment or always accompanying.
  • 砑绢防 - A type of silk hat or headgear.
  • 摘红槿花 - Means to pick a red hibiscus flower.
  • 定头项难 - Refers to a difficult dance move where the head and neck must remain still.
  • 姿质明莹 - Describes someone as having clear and radiant features.
  • 非人间人 - Means not an ordinary human being.
  • 神仙谪坠 - Refers to a divine being who has descended to Earth.
  • 谦谢 - Means to humbly thank or to show gratitude.
  • 短斥 - Means to criticize lightly or to make a mild rebuke.
  • 阿瞒 - A nickname for Emperor Xuanzong, often used in a playful or affectionate manner.
  • 阿暪 - Another variant of '阿瞒'.
  • 相师 - Means to be a model or to serve as an example.
  • 英特越逸之气 - Describes someone as having extraordinary and transcendent qualities.
  • 深沈包育之度 - Means a deep and encompassing demeanor or a profound and nurturing character.
  • 秀迈人 - Describes someone as outstanding and distinguished.
  • 举止闲雅 - Means demeanor is graceful and refined.
  • 公卿间令誉 - Refers to a good reputation among high-ranking officials.
  • 俊迈 - Describes someone as brilliant and outstanding.
  • 正弄 - Refers to a specific piece of music or a formal performance.
  • 待诏出去 - Means to dismiss the musician and send him away.
  • 解秽 - Means to cleanse or to purify.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宁王 - Refers to Prince Ning, a son of Emperor Xuanzong, whose full name was Li Chengyou.
  • 汝阳王璡 - Refers to Li Xian, the eldest son of Prince Ning, who was known for his beauty and musical talent.
  • 阿瞒 - A nickname for Emperor Xuanzong, often used in a playful or affectionate manner, derived from the name of Cao Cao, a famous warlord from the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 阿暪 - Another variant of '阿瞒'.
  • 相师 - In this context, it means to be a model or to serve as an example.
  • 英特越逸之气 - Describes someone as having extraordinary and transcendent qualities, often associated with the ideal ruler.
  • 深沈包育之度 - Refers to a deep and encompassing demeanor, often associated with the ideal ruler or noble person.
  • 秀迈人 - Describes someone as outstanding and distinguished, often used to praise a person's exceptional qualities.
  • 公卿间令誉 - Refers to a good reputation among high-ranking officials, indicating a person's status and respect in society.
  • 俊迈 - Describes someone as brilliant and outstanding, often used to praise a person's exceptional qualities.
  • 正弄 - Refers to a specific piece of music or a formal performance, often used in court music.
  • 待诏出去 - Means to dismiss the musician and send him away, indicating the emperor's displeasure with the performance.
  • 解秽 - Means to cleanse or to purify, often used in the context of music or dance to indicate a cleansing or purifying effect.

Proper Nouns

  • 宁王 - Prince Ning, a son of Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 汝阳王璡 - Li Xian, the eldest son of Prince Ning.
  • 阿瞒 - A nickname for Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 阿暪 - Another variant of '阿瞒'.
  • 正弄 - A specific piece of music or a formal performance.
  • 待诏 - A term used to dismiss a musician or official.
  • 内官 - A term referring to court officials or eunuchs.
  • 花奴 - A nickname for Li Xian, meaning 'flower slave' or 'flower boy'.

[2427]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 黄幡绰亦知音 - Introduces the subject and his skill in music.
  • 上曾使人召之不时至上怒络绎遣使寻捕之 - Describes the sequence of events: Huang Fan Chuo's delay and the emperor's frustration.
  • 曲罢复改曲才三数十声绰即走入 - Describes the musical performance and Huang Fan Chuo's sudden departure.
  • 頼稍迟我向来怒意至必祸焉 - Expresses the emperor's relief that Huang Fan Chuo did not arrive earlier.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 黄幡绰 - A historical figure known for his wit and musical talent during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 上 - Refers to the emperor, a central figure in the narrative.
  • 谒者 - A gatekeeper or official responsible for receiving visitors.
  • 金鸡 - A term for a rooster, often used in imperial contexts for timekeeping or ceremonial purposes.

Proper Nouns

  • 黄幡绰 - The name of the historical figure.
  • 上 - The emperor.
  • 谒者 - The gatekeeper.
  • 金鸡 - The rooster.

[2428]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 耿介不羣 - Upright and unyielding, not conforming to the crowd; a person of high moral character.
  • 深好声乐 - Deeply loves music and sound; has a strong appreciation for music.
  • 尤善羯皷 - Especially skilled in Jiegu; particularly skilled in playing the Jiegu drum.
  • 撚小碧上掌下须有朋【去声】肯声 - When the small green (material) is gripped, the sound produced on the upper palm and lower must be clear and resonant.
  • 汉震第一皷 - The finest drum of the Han Zhen type.
  • 磉用石末花瓮 - The base is made of stone powder and flower jars.
  • 掌不朋【去声】肯声 - The palm does not produce a clear and resonant sound.
  • 非羯皷明矣 - It is clearly not the Jiegu drum.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宋璟 - A Tang Dynasty official and statesman, known for his integrity and political reforms.
  • 羯皷 - A type of drum with origins in the northern regions, possibly from the Jie people (羯族), a nomadic group.
  • 汉震 - Possibly a type of drum or a reference to a historical standard drum used in the Han dynasty.
  • 尊贤里 - A district in Luoyang, the eastern capital of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 郑叔明 - A historical figure, possibly a relative of Song Jing.
  • 开府 - A title or honorific, meaning 'to establish a government office' or 'to be granted the status of a governor.'
  • 东都留守 - 'Governor of the Eastern Capital' — the eastern capital of the Tang was Luoyang.
  • 羯皷録 - Likely the title of a source or record about the Jiegu drum.

Proper Nouns

  • 宋璟 - Tang Dynasty official.
  • 羯皷 - A type of drum, possibly from northern ethnic groups.
  • 汉震 - Possibly a type of drum or historical standard.
  • 开府 - A title or honorific.
  • 东都留守 - Governor of the Eastern Capital.
  • 郑叔明 - A historical figure.
  • 尊贤里 - A district in Luoyang.
  • 郑氏第 - Residence of the Zheng family.
  • 羯皷録 - Title of the source text.
  • 宋夫人 - Lady Song, Song Jing's wife.

[2429]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 对曰臣打五十枚 - Direct quote from Li Guo.
  • 讫上曰汝殊未我打却三竖柜也 - Comparison made by Emperor Xuanzong.
  • 后数年又闻打一竖柜 - Time lapse and a new event.
  • 精于噐用 - Expertise in using tools or instruments.
  • 为荆州节度使 - Position held by Prince Gao.
  • 欲求通谒 - Intention of the traveler.
  • 观者讶之 - Reaction of observers.
  • 岂足尚耶 - Rhetorical question.
  • 士曰但启之尚书当解矣 - Response from the traveler.
  • 指其刚匀之状 - Description of the object.
  • 或腹非之 - Indicates dissent.
  • 命取食柈自选其极平者 - Action taken by Prince Gao.
  • 遂重二卷于柈心 - Another action.
  • 不然无以至此 - Logical statement.
  • 问其所自 - Inquiry.
  • 众方深伏 - Reaction of the audience.
  • 广德中蜀客前双流县丞李琬者亦能之 - Introduces another character.
  • 调集至长安居务本里 - Change in location.
  • 尝夜闻羯皷曲颇工妙 - Describes an experience.
  • 谓皷工曰君所击者岂非耶婆色鸡乎 - Question to the drummer.
  • 虽至精能而无尾何也 - Follow-up question.
  • 工大异之曰君固知音者 - Response from the drummer.
  • 此事无有知某太常工人也 - Statement by the drummer.
  • 近者张儒入长安其家流散 - Recent event.
  • 父没河西此曲遂绝 - Continuation of the background story.
  • 今但按旧 - Final statement.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太常 - The imperial music bureau responsible for managing court music and ceremonies.
  • 广德 - A reign title of Emperor Suzong of the Tang Dynasty, indicating a specific period in history.
  • 双流县丞 - A local official position in the Tang Dynasty, responsible for administrative duties.

[2430]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 谱数本寻之竟无结尾之声因夜夜求之也 - He searched for the score repeatedly, yet there was no sound of the ending, so he sought it every night.
  • 曲下意尽乎 - Is the meaning of the piece exhausted?
  • 可言矣夫曲有如此者须以他曲解之方可尽其声也 - It can be said that for some pieces, one must use another piece to resolve them in order to fully express their sound.
  • 夫耶婆娑鸡当用屈柘急遍解 - For the piece 'Yebaosajiji,' one should use quick and urgent passages to resolve it.
  • 工泣而谢之 - The artisan wept and thanked him.
  • 即言于寺卿奏为主簿 - Immediately spoke to the temple official, who appointed him as the head clerk.
  • 累官至太常少卿宗正卿 - Gradually promoted to the position of Deputy Chief of the Imperial Music Bureau and Chief of the Imperial Clan Affairs Office.
  • 代宗朝宰相杜鸿渐亦能羯皷 - During the reign of Emperor Daizong, the chancellor Du Hongjian could also play the羯皷.
  • 永太中为三副元帅西川节度使 - During the Yongtai period, he served as the third deputy commander and governor of the Western Sichuan region.
  • 至成都李琬有削杖者在蜀以杖献 - Upon arriving in Chengdu, Li Wan had a carved stick, which he presented in Sichuan.
  • 鸿渐以示于众曰此尤物也 - Du Hongjian showed it to the public and said, 'This is a rare object.'
  • 四山猿鸟皆惊飞噭走 - The apes and birds in the mountains all flew away in alarm.
  • 昔防之 - In ancient times, the people were cautious.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 羯皷 - A type of drum used in traditional Chinese music, particularly in the Tang Dynasty.
  • 杜鸿渐 - A prominent chancellor during the reign of Emperor Daizong of Tang, known for his political and military achievements.
  • 李琬 - A historical figure, possibly a local official or noble, who presented a carved stick to Du Hongjian.
  • 屈柘急遍解 - A specific musical technique involving quick and urgent passages to resolve a piece.
  • 太常少卿 - A high-ranking official in the Imperial Music Bureau, responsible for music and rituals.
  • 宗正卿 - The head of the Imperial Clan Affairs Office, responsible for managing the imperial family's affairs.
  • 永太中 - The Yongtai period, a time during the reign of Emperor Daizong of Tang.
  • 西川节度使 - The governor of the Western Sichuan region, responsible for military and administrative duties.
  • 成都 - A major city in Sichuan, historically significant for its cultural and economic importance.
  • 削杖 - A carved stick, often used as a symbol of authority or a musical instrument.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 谱数本寻之竟无结尾之声因夜夜求之也 - This sentence is a compound structure where '谱数本寻之' is the main action, and '竟无结尾之声因夜夜求之也' is a result clause. It can be translated as 'He searched for the score repeatedly, yet there was no sound of the ending, so he sought it every night.'
  • 曲下意尽乎工曰尽琬曰意尽即曲尽又何索焉 - This sentence is a series of questions and answers. '曲下意尽乎' is a question, '工曰尽' is the artisan's response, and '琬曰意尽即曲尽又何索焉' is Du Hongjian's response. It can be translated as 'Is the meaning of the piece exhausted? The artisan said yes. Du Hongjian said, 'If the meaning is exhausted, then the piece is exhausted. What else do we need to seek?'
  • 可言矣夫曲有如此者须以他曲解之方可尽其声也 - This sentence is a statement followed by an explanation. '可言矣' is the statement, and '夫曲有如此者须以他曲解之方可尽其声也' is the explanation. It can be translated as 'It can be said that for some pieces, one must use another piece to resolve them in order to fully express their sound.'
  • 夫耶婆娑鸡当用屈柘急遍解 - This sentence is a specific instruction. '夫耶婆娑鸡' is the subject, and '当用屈柘急遍解' is the instruction. It can be translated as 'For the piece 'Yebaosajiji,' one should use quick and urgent passages to resolve it.'
  • 工泣而谢之即言于寺卿奏为主簿 - This sentence is a sequence of actions. '工泣而谢之' is the first action, and '即言于寺卿奏为主簿' is the second action. It can be translated as 'The artisan wept and thanked him, and immediately spoke to the temple official, who appointed him as the head clerk.'
  • 累官至太常少卿宗正卿 - This sentence is a sequence of promotions. '累官' is the verb, and '至太常少卿宗正卿' is the result. It can be translated as 'He was gradually promoted to the position of Deputy Chief of the Imperial Music Bureau and Chief of the Imperial Clan Affairs Office.'
  • 代宗朝宰相杜鸿渐亦能羯皷 - This sentence is a statement about Du Hongjian's skills. '代宗朝宰相杜鸿渐' is the subject, and '亦能羯皷' is the predicate. It can be translated as 'During the reign of Emperor Daizong, the chancellor Du Hongjian could also play the羯皷.'
  • 永太中为三副元帅西川节度使 - This sentence is a statement about Du Hongjian's roles. '永太中' is the time, and '为三副元帅西川节度使' is the roles. It can be translated as 'During the Yongtai period, he served as the third deputy commander and governor of the Western Sichuan region.'
  • 至成都李琬有削杖者在蜀以杖献 - This sentence is a statement about an event. '至成都' is the location, '李琬有削杖者' is the subject, and '在蜀以杖献' is the action. It can be translated as 'Upon arriving in Chengdu, Li Wan had a carved stick, which he presented in Sichuan.'
  • 鸿渐以示于众曰此尤物也 - This sentence is a statement about Du Hongjian's action. '鸿渐以示于众' is the action, and '曰此尤物也' is the statement. It can be translated as 'Du Hongjian showed it to the public and said, 'This is a rare object.'

[2431]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 拊百獸舞庭 - Literal phrase meaning 'patting a hundred beasts to dance in the courtyard,' likely a metaphorical expression referring to a performance or display involving animals.
  • 此豈逺耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Is this far away?' used to emphasize the closeness or accessibility of something.
  • 若某于此稍曾致力 - Humble expression meaning 'If I had put any effort into this,' indicating the speaker's modesty.
  • 猶未臻妙 - Means 'still not reaching perfection' or 'not yet achieving excellence.'
  • 尚能及是 - Means 'still able to reach this level' or 'still capable of achieving this.'
  • 何有不感 - Means 'how could there not be a response?' or 'how could there not be an effect?'
  • 稍曾致力 - Means 'put any effort into' or 'tried to do.'
  • 疾徐髙下而節之 - Means 'by varying the speed, height, and rhythm,' referring to the control of a musical performance.
  • 無不應之而變旋 - Means 'nothing fails to respond and change accordingly,' describing a harmonious reaction to the music.
  • 唯仆射韓臯善亦不甚露 - Means 'only the Chancellor Han Gao was good at it but not very public about it,' indicating a rare skill that was not widely known.
  • 鄂州節度使時聞于黄樓一兩習而已 - Refers to 'when he was the Governor of Ezhou, he was heard practicing once or twice at Huangge Tower,' indicating a specific instance of the skill being demonstrated.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鸿渐曰 - Refers to a person named Hong Jian, likely a historical figure or a character in a story.
  • 至圣遇夫贤臣 - Refers to a wise ruler meeting with a virtuous minister, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 花岩阁 - Refers to a specific location, likely a famous or significant place in the context of the story.
  • 仆射韩臯 - Refers to Han Gao, a historical figure who held the position of Chancellor (仆射) during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 鄂州节度使 - Refers to the Governor of Ezhou, a military and administrative position during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 黄楼 - Refers to Huangge Tower, a specific location where the skill was demonstrated.

Proper Nouns

  • 鸿渐 - The name of the speaker or narrator.
  • 韩臯 - The name of the Chancellor (仆射) who was skilled in the performance.
  • 鄂州 - The name of the region where Han Gao served as Governor.
  • 黄楼 - The name of the specific location where the skill was demonstrated.

[2432]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 蛮夷之乐 - Refers to the music of the barbarian tribes, often used to denote music from non-Han Chinese regions.
  • 铜皷 - A type of drum made of bronze, commonly used in southern regions.
  • 形如腰皷 - Describes the shape of the drum as resembling a waist drum.
  • 一头有面 - Indicates that one end of the drum has a surface or face.
  • 面与身连 - States that the face and body of the drum are connected as one piece.
  • 全用铜铸 - Means the drum is entirely made of bronze.
  • 遍有虫鱼花草之状 - Describes the surface of the drum as being decorated with various patterns of insects, fish, flowers, and plants.
  • 通体均匀厚二分以来 - Indicates that the drum is uniformly thick, about two fen (an ancient Chinese unit of length).
  • 炉铸之妙实为竒巧 - Praises the skillful casting of the drum, emphasizing its craftsmanship.
  • 撃之响亮不下鸣鼍 - States that when struck, the drum produces a sound as loud as that of a crocodile (鼍, a type of crocodile).
  • 贞元中骠国进乐 - Refers to the period during the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang (779-805) when the kingdom of Piao (present-day Myanmar) sent musicians to the Tang court.
  • 玊螺 - Refers to a type of shell, specifically a white conch shell.
  • 皆螺之白者 - Clarifies that the shells are white conch shells.
  • 非琢玊所为也 - Emphasizes that these shells are not carved from jade.
  • 南蛮酋首之家 - Refers to the homes of the leaders of the southern barbarian tribes.
  • 咸通末 - Refers to the end of the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (859-873).
  • 幽州张方 - Refers to Zhang Xianfang, a person from Youzhou (present-day Beijing).
  • 贬龚州刺史 - Indicates that Zhang Xianfang was demoted to the position of governor of Gongzhou (present-day Guangxi).
  • 到任后修葺州城 - States that after taking office, Zhang Xianfang repaired the city walls.
  • 因掘土得一铜皷 - Describes how he found a bronze drum while digging soil.
  • 满任载以归京 - Means he took the drum back to the capital when his term ended.
  • 到襄汉以为无用之物 - States that upon arriving in Xiangyang, he considered the drum useless.
  • 遂舍延夀庆院 - Indicates that he donated the drum to the Yanshou Qingshan Monastery.
  • 用大木鱼悬于斋室 - Describes how the drum was hung in a meditation room using a large wooden fish.
  • 今见存焉 - States that the drum is still present today.
  • 僖宗朝 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Xizong of Tang (873-888).
  • 郑续镇番禺日 - Refers to the time when Zheng Xu was stationed in Fanyu (present-day Guangzhou).
  • 牧儿因放牛闻田中有蛤鸣 - States that a herder heard a frog croaking in a field while grazing cattle.
  • 牧童遂捕之 - Indicates that the herder then caught the frog.
  • 蛤跳入一穴 - Describes the frog jumping into a hole.
  • 掘之深大即蛮酋塜也 - States that the hole was deep and large, revealing it to be the tomb of a barbarian chief.
  • 蛤乃无踪 - Indicates that the frog disappeared.
  • 穴中得一铜皷 - Describes finding a bronze drum in the hole.
  • 其色翠緑 - States that the drum was a bright green color.
  • 土蚀数处损缺 - Indicates that the drum had several areas eroded by soil, causing damage.
  • 其上隐起多铸蛙黾之状 - Describes the surface of the drum as having many raised patterns of frogs and toads.
  • 疑其鸣蛤即皷精也 - Suggests that the croaking frog might be a spirit of the drum.
  • 遂状其缘由纳于广帅 - Indicates that the herder reported the incident to the governor of Guangzhou.
  • 悬于武库 - Describes how the drum was hung in the armory.
  • 今尚存焉 - States that the drum is still present today.
  • 出岭表録异 - Refers to the book *Lingbiao Lüyi* (Records of Strange Things from Beyond the Five Ridges), a collection of strange tales from southern China.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蛮夷 - Refers to non-Han Chinese peoples, often used in a derogatory sense.
  • 贞元 - The era name of Emperor Dezong of Tang (779-805).
  • 骠国 - The kingdom of Piao, located in present-day Myanmar, known for its musical traditions.
  • 咸通 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (859-873).
  • 幽州 - An ancient region in present-day Beijing.
  • 龚州 - An ancient state in present-day Guangxi.
  • 襄汉 - Refers to Xiangyang, a city in present-day Hubei.
  • 延夀庆院 - The name of a monastery in Xiangyang.
  • 僖宗 - The era name of Emperor Xizong of Tang (873-888).
  • 番禺 - An ancient name for Guangzhou, the capital of Guangdong province.
  • 岭表 - Refers to the regions south of the Five Ridges, including present-day Guangdong, Guangxi, and Yunnan provinces.

Proper Nouns

  • 贞元 - The era name of Emperor Dezong of Tang (779-805).
  • 骠国 - The kingdom of Piao, located in present-day Myanmar.
  • 咸通 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (859-873).
  • 幽州 - An ancient region in present-day Beijing.
  • 龚州 - An ancient state in present-day Guangxi.
  • 襄汉 - Refers to Xiangyang, a city in present-day Hubei.
  • 延夀庆院 - The name of a monastery in Xiangyang.
  • 僖宗 - The era name of Emperor Xizong of Tang (873-888).
  • 番禺 - An ancient name for Guangzhou, the capital of Guangdong province.
  • 岭表 - Refers to the regions south of the Five Ridges, including present-day Guangdong, Guangxi, and Yunnan provinces.

[2433]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 太宗时西国进一胡善弹琵琶 - During the reign of Emperor Taizong, a foreigner from the West skilled in playing the pipa was brought in.
  • 作一曲琵琶拨倍麤 - He played a piece where the strings and plectrum were unusually thick.
  • 上每不欲番人胜中国 - The emperor always did not want foreigners to surpass the Chinese.
  • 乃置酒髙防使罗黑黑隔帷之一遍而得 - Therefore, he arranged a banquet and had Luo Heihei play behind a curtain.
  • 谓胡人曰此曲吾宫人能之 - He told the foreigner that this piece could be played by a palace lady.
  • 取大琵琶遂于帷下令黑黑弹之 - He took out a large pipa and ordered Luo Heihei to play it.
  • 不遗一字 - Not a single note was omitted.
  • 胡人谓是宫女也 - The foreigner thought it was a palace lady.
  • 惊叹辞去 - He was amazed and left.
  • 西国闻之降者数十国 - Western countries heard about it and submitted, bringing dozens of nations under submission.
  • 贞观中弹琵琶裴洛儿始废拨用手 - During the Zhenguan era, Pei Luer began to play the pipa without using a plectrum but with his hand.
  • 今俗所谓搊琵琶是也 - This is now known as 'fou' pipa.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 汲书 - Ancient bamboo slips found in a tomb, often containing texts from the Warring States period. The term '汲书' specifically indicates that these texts were discovered in a well-preserved state.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '智永尝于楼上学书业成方下' emphasizes that Zhi Yong only descended after completing his studies, highlighting his dedication and perseverance in calligraphy.
  • The sentence '梁周兴嗣编次千字文' indicates that Zhou Xingsi compiled the Thousand Character Text, a famous Chinese primer.
  • The sentence '右军孙智永禅师自临八百夲' highlights Zhi Yong's skill and diligence in calligraphy, as he personally copied eight hundred volumes.
  • The sentence '散与人外江南诸寺各留一夲' describes Zhi Yong's distribution of his calligraphy works to temples outside.
  • The sentence '永公住吴兴永欣寺' refers to Zhi Yong's residence in the Yongxin Temple in Wu County, reflecting his high status in the Buddhist community.
  • The sentence '积学书后有秃笔头十瓮' describes the large number of used pen tips Zhi Yong had accumulated, reflecting his dedication to calligraphy.
  • The sentence '每瓮皆数千人来觅书并请题额者如市' describes the large number of people seeking his calligraphy, likening it to a market.
  • The sentence '所居户限为穿穴乃用铁叶裹之谓为铁门限' describes the popularity of Zhi Yong's calligraphy works, as the threshold of his residence was worn down and had to be wrapped in iron leaf.
  • The sentence '后取笔头瘗之号为退笔塚' describes the place where Zhi Yong buried his used pen tips, reflecting his love and dedication to calligraphy.
  • The sentence '自制铭志' describes Zhi Yong's creation of inscriptions, reflecting his dedication to calligraphy.
  • The sentence '常居永欣寺阁上临书所退笔头置之于大竹簏' describes Zhi Yong's habit of placing his used pen tips in a large bamboo basket, reflecting his dedication to calligraphy.
  • The sentence '簏受一石余而五簏皆满' describes the large capacity of the bamboo basket, reflecting the large number of used pen tips Zhi Yong had accumulated.
  • The sentence '隋永欣寺僧智果防稽人也' describes Zhi Guo's background as a monk from Fangji County.
  • The sentence '工书铭石' describes Zhi Guo's skill in calligraphy and stone carving.
  • The sentence '为痩健造次难类' describes the difficulty of imitating Zhi Guo's calligraphy style, which is thin and strong.
  • The sentence '尝谓永师云和尚得右军肉智果得骨' describes the comparison between Zhi Yong and Zhi Guo's calligraphy styles, with Zhi Yong inheriting the 'flesh' and Zhi Guo the 'bone' of Wang Xizhi's style.
  • The sentence '筋骨藏于肤肉山水不厌高深而此公稍乏清幽伤于浅露' describes the balance between strength and subtlety in calligraphy, with Zhi Guo's style being slightly lacking in depth and subtlety.
  • The sentence '若吴人之战轻进易退勇力而非武虚张夸耀无乃小人儒乎' describes Zhi Guo's calligraphy style as being too bold and lacking in depth, comparing it to the light and shallow style of Wu people.
  • The sentence '智果隶行草入能' describes Zhi Guo's proficiency in various styles of calligraphy, including clerical, running, and cursive script.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 王羲之兰亭序 - Wang Xizhi's *Lanting Xu* is one of the most famous calligraphy works in Chinese history, known for its beauty and historical significance.
  • 僧智永弟子辨才 - Biancai was a disciple of Zhizhong, a renowned calligrapher and the son of Wang Xizhi. He is known for his skill in calligraphy and for having the *Lanting Xu*.
  • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang (Li Shimin) was a prominent ruler of the Tang Dynasty, known for his military prowess and cultural patronage.
  • 听政之暇 - Means 'listening to government affairs,' referring to the time when the emperor was not occupied with state affairs, implying leisure time.
  • 真草书帖 - Means 'true script and cursive script,' two styles of calligraphy. True script is more formal, while cursive script is more fluid and artistic.
  • 搆募备尽 - Reflects the high value placed on the *Lanting Xu* and the lengths to which people would go to obtain it.
  • 内道塲 - The inner Buddhist temple was a place of high prestige and privilege, where only trusted individuals were allowed.
  • 恩赉优洽数日后 - Highlights the generous treatment given to the monk, reflecting the emperor's respect and desire to obtain the *Lanting Xu*.
  • 善诱无所不至 - Shows the emperor's persistence and diplomatic approach in trying to obtain the *Lanting Xu*.
  • 辨才确称往日侍奉先师实常获见 - Provides insight into the monk's past relationship with the *Lanting Xu* and his role in preserving it.
  • 自师没后荐经丧乱坠失不知所在 - Explains the loss of the *Lanting Xu* and the monk's uncertainty about its whereabouts.
  • 遂放归越中 - Indicates the monk's release back to his home region, suggesting that the emperor's efforts to obtain the *Lanting Xu* were not successful.
  • 又勅追辨才入内重问兰亭 - Shows the emperor's continued interest in obtaining the *Lanting Xu* and his persistence in trying to persuade the monk.
  • 此僧耆年又无所用 - Reflects the emperor's frustration with the monk's old age and perceived lack of usefulness.
  • 若得一智略之士设 - Suggests the emperor's openness to new strategies and the idea that a wise and strategic person might help in obtaining the *Lanting Xu*.

  • 辨才 - A monk and disciple of Zhizhong, known for his skill in calligraphy and possession of the *Lanting Xu*.

  • 太宗初怒老僧之秘恡 - Emperor Taizong was initially angry at the monk's greed or stinginess.
  • 俄以其年耄不忍加刑 - Soon, Emperor Taizong, moved by the monk's age, spared him from punishment.
  • 數月后仍赐物三千段谷三千石 - Several months later, Emperor Taizong still granted the monk 3,000 pieces of cloth and 3,000 stones of grain.
  • 便勅越州支给 - The emperor ordered the state of Yue to provide the goods.
  • 辨才不敢将入己用 - The monk did not dare to take the gifts for himself.
  • 墖甚精防至今犹存 - The pagoda is very finely crafted and still exists to this day.
  • 老僧因惊悸患重不能彊饭唯歠粥歳余乃卒 - The old monk was so frightened that he fell ill, could not force himself to eat, only drank porridge, and died after more than a year.
  • 帝命供奉搨书人赵模韩道政冯承素诸葛真等四人各搨数夲以赐皇太子诸王近臣 - The emperor ordered the imperial copyists, Zhao Mo, Han Daoling, Feng Chengsu, and Zhuge Zhen, to each copy several copies of the *Lantingji Xu* to be given to the crown prince, princes, and close ministers.
  • 贞观二十三年圣躬不豫幸玉华宫含风殿临崩谓髙宗曰吾欲从汝求一物汝诚孝也岂能违吾心耶汝意何如 - On the verge of death, Emperor Taizong told Emperor Gaozong that he wanted to ask for something, emphasizing his filial piety and asking for his opinion.
  • 吾所欲得兰亭可与我将去后随仙驾入宫矣 - Emperor Taizong wanted the *Lantingji Xu* to be taken with him after his death to the Xuan Palace.
  • 今赵模等所搨在者一夲尚钱数万也 - The copies made by Zhao Mo et al. still exist and are very valuable.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太宗 - Refers to Emperor Taizong of Tang, one of the most famous emperors in Chinese history.
  • 老僧 - Likely refers to a monk named Biancai, who was involved in the story of the *Lantingji Xu*.
  • 赵模韩道政冯承素诸葛真 - These are the names of the imperial copyists who made copies of the *Lantingji Xu*.
  • 贞观二十三年 - The year 649 AD, when Emperor Taizong was on his deathbed.
  • 玉华宫 - The Yuhua Palace, a palace where Emperor Taizong spent his last days.
  • 含风殿 - The Huanfeng Hall, a hall within the Yuhua Palace.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 少有盛名 - Common phrase meaning 'had a great reputation early in life.'
  • 風流髙迈 - Describes a person as having a distinguished and refined character, often associated with elegance and intellectual prowess.
  • 草隶继父之美妙于画 - Complex phrase indicating that Wang Xizhi inherited his father's excellence in both cursive and clerical script and was also skilled in painting.
  • 误落笔就成乌駮牸牛极妙绝 - Literally means 'mistakenly drew a black and white spotted cow which turned out to be extremely wonderful.' Highlights the accidental yet masterful creation of an artwork.
  • 书駮牛赋于扇上 - Means 'wrote a poem about the spotted cow on the fan,' showcasing Wang Xizhi's ability to combine visual art with literary expression.
  • 此扇义熈中犹在 - Indicates that this fan still existed during the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song (Yi Xi period).
  • 多才气尤攻丹青 - Means 'possessed great talent, especially excelled in painting,' emphasizing Gu Kaizhi's versatility and skill in the arts.
  • 写形势莫不妙绝 - Translates to 'depicting scenes, none were not superb,' highlighting the quality of his work.
  • 卿画自生人已来未有 - Means 'Your painting is unparalleled since the beginning of humanity,' a high compliment from Xie An.
  • 卿画苍苍古来未有 - Similar to the previous phrase, it means 'Your painting of the vast sky is unprecedented in ancient times.'
  • 曾以一厨画暂寄桓皆其妙迹所珍秘者 - Indicates that Gu Kaizhi once entrusted a collection of his finest works to Huan Xuan for safekeeping.
  • 封题之其后开取之诳云不开 - Means 'Huan Xuan later opened the sealed collection and claimed he did not open it,' suggesting a playful or deceptive act.
  • 妙画通神变化飞去 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the masterpieces have transcended the physical realm, as if they have flown away,' implying the extraordinary quality and spiritual nature of the paintings.
  • 人之登仙也 - Means 'like a person ascending to immortality,' another metaphor for the transcendent quality of the art.
  • 才绝画绝痴绝 - Describes Gu Kaizhi as exceptional in three areas: talent, painting, and eccentricity.
  • 悦一邻女乃画女于壁当心钉之 - Means 'he liked a neighboring girl and painted her on the wall, then nailed it in her heart,' a humorous and somewhat bizarre anecdote.
  • 女患心痛告于长康康遂防钉乃愈 - Means 'the girl suffered from heart pain and told Gu Kaizhi, who then removed the nail, and she recovered,' a whimsical story that highlights the power of art.
  • 欲写殷仲堪真仲堪素有目疾固辞 - Means 'he wanted to paint a portrait of Yin Zhongkan, but Yin refused because he had eye problems.'
  • 明府无病若明防瞳子飞白拂上便如轻云蔽日 - Playful response, meaning 'If you have no illness, then if I add a fly white (a light stroke) over your pupils, it will look like a light cloud covering the sun.'
  • 颊上乃加三毛云楷俊朗有鉴识具 - Means 'he added three hairs to the cheek of Pei Kai, saying that Pei was handsome, intelligent, and had good judgment,' another humorous anecdote that showcases Gu Kaizhi's wit and creativity.

  • 眉麤眼竪发如锥 - Thick eyebrows, upright eyes, and hair like a cone; describes a fierce and intense appearance.
  • 怪异令人不可知 - Strange and mysterious, making it impossible to know; suggests a sense of mystery and the unknown.
  • 科头巨卒欲生鬼 - The bald-headed giant soldier seems to be giving birth to a ghost; could be a metaphorical or symbolic description.
  • 半面女郎安小儿 - A half-faced girl is holding a child; describes a unique and possibly symbolic scene.
  • 况闻此寺初兴置 - Moreover, hearing that this temple was initially established; suggests a historical context.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 笔迹遒健精彩欲活 - Descriptive phrase about the vitality and skill in the brushwork; suggests the images appear almost alive.
  • 心竦神悸足不能进 - Poetic way of describing the emotional impact of the painting, causing the viewer to feel awe and hesitation.
  • 竒絶也 - Classical expression meaning 'truly extraordinary' or 'a marvel.'
  • 非神仙之人无以冩神仙之质也 - Philosophical statement that only someone with a divine nature can portray the essence of immortals; uses the classical structure '非...无以...'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 西蜀道士张素卿 - Zhang Suxiang, a Daoist painter from Western Shu (modern Sichuan province) during the Later Shu dynasty, known for religious and mythological paintings.
  • 青城山丈人观 - Zhangmen Temple on Qingcheng Mountain, a famous Daoist site in Sichuan, associated with the legendary Daoist sage Zhang Daoling.
  • 五岳四渎真形 - Refers to the 'True Forms' of the Five Sacred Mountains (Wuyue) and the Four Great Rivers (Sida), important in Daoist cosmology.
  • 十二溪女 - Possibly a reference to the Twelve Stream Nymphs, a Daoist or mythological concept, though this is less well-documented and may be a local or textual variation.
  • 蜀主 - Refers to the ruler of the Later Shu kingdom, likely Emperor Meng Chang.
  • 秘书少监黄筌 - Huang Qian, a famous painter and official during the Later Shu dynasty, known for his court painting style.
  • 伪翰林学士欧阳炯 - Ou Yangjiong, a翰林学士 (Imperial Academy scholar) during the Later Shu dynasty; '伪' may indicate that this was during the period of the Later Shu, which was considered a 'false' or 'usurped' dynasty by the Northern Song.
  • 水部贠外郎黄居宝 - Huang Jubao, an official in the Water Department (水部), and a 贠外郎 was an official rank in the Tang and later dynasties.
  • 八分题之 - Refers to the 八分书 (Ba Fen shu), a classical Chinese calligraphy style, often used for inscriptions and formal writing.
  • 三絶 - The 'Three Perfections' – a classical Chinese concept referring to the highest level of excellence in poetry, calligraphy, and painting.
  • 八仙 - The Eight Immortals, a well-known Daoist group in Chinese mythology; the list provided in the text is: 李已、容成、董仲舒、张道陵、严君平、李八百、长寿、葛永璝, which differs slightly from the more commonly known list.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 于越州沈七处卜得纯乾卦 - Wang Zhu consulted with a diviner named Shen Qi in Yue Prefecture and received the 'pure Qian' hexagram.
  • 下四位动变观卦 - The lower four lines of the Qian hexagram changed, transforming it into the Guan hexagram.
  • 公今应举得此卦观国之光利用宾于王本是嘉兆 - The diviner interpreted the Guan hexagram as a sign of seeing the light of the state and being useful to the king, which is a good omen.
  • 然交动羣隂咸阳下成乾卦上变至四又不至五五是君位未得利见大人 - However, the interaction of the yin lines caused the hexagram to change, but it did not reach the fifth line, which represents the position of the king, indicating that Wang Zhu would not meet the king.
  • 恐公此行不至京而回 - The diviner feared that Wang Zhu would not reach the capital and would return.

  • 颍隂日者 - A diviner in Yingyang.
  • 陈防为颍隂太守属安禄山反 - Chen Fang, the governor of Yingyang, faced the rebellion of An Lushan.

  • 澍闻便欲至薄晩出城走 - Chen Fang heard about this and immediately fled the city at dusk.
  • 录事参军扣马令回 - The clerk of the court stopped Chen Fang and ordered him to return.

  • 从今五日当有家便来取公然有五百车禄在必亦不死 - The diviner predicted that within five days, someone would come to take him away, and he would have five hundred carts of wealth, meaning he would not die.
  • 至七日食时公无恙矣然当去此求住不得 - On the seventh day, at mealtime, Chen Fang was fine, but he could not stay there.

  • 至夜后伪作敇书追入京令向西两驿上差人逆来夜半敕书至明早召集诸官宣敕便令手刃就馆中诛杀孙并手刃二十余人 - At night, Chen Fang forged an imperial edict, ordering Sun to be summoned to the capital. He sent people to intercept Sun on the road, and at midnight, the fake edict arrived. In the morning, officials were gathered, and Chen Fang ordered Sun and twenty others to be killed.
  • 杀录事参军其孙尉先令入取妻及女等夜半齐到明日平明尽杀之令上住知州事便发入京以官绢五匹赏卜者 - The clerk of the court was also killed. Sun's wife and daughters were brought in at midnight, and in the morning, they were all killed. Chen Fang then took control of the state affairs and sent an official to the capital, rewarding the diviner with five bolts of official silk.

  • 每清旦布蓍为人决事取资足一日为生则闭斋治园 - This clause describes his daily routine and must be read as a single descriptive statement.
  • 栖岩惊曰家去几何父往矣不然将仆于道老父出栖岩顾百钱乃也 - This clause describes the interaction and the realization of the divination's nature.
  • 因悟其所騐之辰则栖岩甲子乃叹曰 - This clause links the realization to a personal reflection.
  • 吾虽少而治易不自意能幽入鬼鉴死复何恨 - This clause expresses Wang Qianian's philosophical outlook.
  • 乃沐浴更新衣与妻子诀少时而卒 - This clause describes his final actions and death.
  • 赵自勤尝谒选卜于长安县路生 - This clause introduces Zhao Ziqin and his consultation.
  • 路云公之官非重日即是重口 - This clause provides the fortune-teller's prediction.
  • 后六月六日又卜路云公之官九日不出十二日出 - This clause describes a follow-up consultation.
  • 至九日宰相果索吏部由歴至十二日敕出为左拾遗 - This clause describes the fulfillment of the prediction.
  • 拾遗之字各有一口 - This clause explains the significance of the character.
  • 又阙王晃七月内访卜于路生 - This clause introduces another consultation.
  • 路云九月当入省官有礼字 - This clause provides another prediction.
  • 时礼部贠外陶翰在座乃曰公即是仆替人 - This clause describes the interaction with Tao Han.
  • 九月陶病请假敕除王礼部贠外 - This clause describes the outcome of the prediction.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '君用思精宻而韵不能髙将为良医矣' is somewhat elliptical and can be interpreted as 'Though you think with precision, your tone cannot be high, you will become an excellent physician.'
  • The sentence '君体有病宜服五石汤不治年及三十当眉落' is elliptical and could be interpreted as 'Your body has a disease; you should take the Five Stones Soup. If you do not treat it, by the time you reach thirty, your eyebrows will fall.'
  • The sentence '佛使我来理君病也' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'The Buddha has sent me to treat your illness.'
  • The sentence '命太医合药言得白髓杂玉与虎魄屑当灭此痕' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'He ordered the imperial physician to prepare medicine, saying that if one could obtain the marrow of the white deer, mixed with jade and tiger bone powder, it would remove this mark.'
  • The sentence '乃合膏虎魄太多及差痕不灭左颊有赤防如痣' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'However, the ointment contained too much tiger bone powder, and when the wound healed, the mark did not disappear. Instead, there was a red mole on the left cheek like a birthmark.'
  • The sentence '忽有一人不自通名径入斋中坐于光禄之侧' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'Suddenly, a person who did not introduce himself entered the study and sat beside the official.'
  • The sentence '因以刀针肿上倐忽之间顿针两脚及膀胱百余下出黄脓水三升许而去' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'He then used a knife and needle to puncture the swollen area. In a sudden moment, he inserted the needles into both legs and the bladder more than a hundred times, extracting about three liters of yellow pus, and then left.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '之人不受厚施唯乞此息肉遂珍重而去' is elliptical and must be read as a single action: 'This person did not accept generous gifts but only asked for this piece of meat and then took it with great care and left.'
  • The sentence '布亦意其賢聖也計僧去五六坊復有一少年美如冠玉騎白馬遂扣其門' is a long sequence of actions and descriptions that must be read as a continuous narrative: 'Bu also thought he was a sage or holy person. After the monk had gone several blocks, there was a young man as beautiful as jade, riding a white horse, who then knocked on the door.'
  • The sentence '適有胡僧到無布遽延入具述胡僧事' is elliptical and must be read as a single action: 'Just now a foreign monk arrived and there was no cloth, so he hurriedly invited him in and told him about the monk's visit.'
  • The sentence '吁嗟不悅曰馬小蹶足竟後此僧' is a sequence of actions and expressions that must be read as a continuous narrative: 'He sighed and said unhappily, 'The horse slightly stumbled and then caught up with this monk.'
  • The sentence '布驚異詰其故' is a sequence of actions and expressions that must be read as a continuous narrative: 'Bu was surprised and asked why.'
  • The sentence '上帝失樂神二人近知藏於君女鼻中我天人也奉命來取不意此僧先取之當獲譴矣' is a long sequence of actions and descriptions that must be read as a continuous narrative: 'The two lost gods of music from heaven recently knew they were hidden in your daughter's nose. I am a celestial being sent to retrieve them; I did not expect this monk to take them first, and I will be punished for it.'
  • The sentence '布方作禮舉手而失' is a sequence of actions and expressions that must be read as a continuous narrative: 'Bu was about to perform a ritual and raised his hand, but it disappeared.'
  • The sentence '荊州處士侯又玄嘗出郊厠於塚上跌傷其肘瘡甚' is a long sequence of actions and descriptions that must be read as a continuous narrative: 'Hou Youxuan, a reclusive scholar from Jingzhou, once went out to the countryside and fell in a tomb, falling and injuring his elbow, which became very sore.'
  • The sentence '逢一老人問何所苦也又玄具言且見其肘' is a long sequence of actions and descriptions that must be read as a continuous narrative: 'He met an old man who asked what was wrong. Hou Youxuan explained in detail and showed his elbow.'
  • The sentence '老人言偶有良藥可封之十日不開必愈又玄如其言及解視一臂遂落' is a long sequence of actions and descriptions that must be read as a continuous narrative: 'The old man said he had a good medicine that could be applied; if it was not removed for ten days, it would heal. Hou Youxuan followed his advice, and when he removed the bandage, one arm had fallen off.'
  • The sentence '又玄兄弟五六人互病病必出血月餘' is a long sequence of actions and descriptions that must be read as a continuous narrative: 'Hou Youxuan and his five or six brothers were all ill, and they bled for more than a month.'
  • The sentence '見兄兩臂忽病瘡六七處小者如榆錢大者如錢皆成人面' is a long sequence of actions and descriptions that must be read as a continuous narrative: 'He saw his brother's two arms suddenly develop sores, six or seven in number, small ones like jujube coins and large ones like copper coins, all forming human faces.'
